title: Conditions for a z test about a proportion
description: Check whether or not the the conditions have been met for a one sample z test for a proportion
topic_path: Setting up a test for a population proportion
Conditions for a z test about a proportion

assessment_items.question_content:
Jules works on a small team of $40$ employees. Each employee receives an annual rating, the best of which is "exceeds expectations." Management claimed that $10\%$ of employees earn this rating, but Jules suspected it was actually less common. She obtained an anonymous random sample of $10$ ratings for employees on her team. She wants to use the sample data to test $H_0:p=0.1$ versus $H_\text{a}:p<0.1$, where $p$ is the proportion of all employees on her team who earned "exceeds expectations."

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did Jules' sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "She obtained an anonymous random sample of $10$ ratings for employees on her team," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=10$ ratings, and we expect $10\%$ of them to be "exceeds expectations," we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=10(0.10)\\\\
&=1 \ngeq 10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=10(0.90)\\\\
&=9 \ngeq 10 
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $1$ "exceeds expectations" rating and $9$ lower ratings, both of which are fewer than $10$, the sample does **not** satisfy the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, we can still treat each observation in the sample as independent as long as we sample less than $10\%$ of the population. Since our sample of $10$ ratings is more than $10\%$ of the $40$ ratings in the population, the sample has **not** met the independence condition.

###Answer

The data is a random sample from the population of interest.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The expected counts of successes and failures are both sufficiently large.
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Abdullah is a quality control expert at a factory that paints car parts. He knows that the factory paints $20\%$ of parts with an error. He recommended a change in the painting process, and he wants to see if this error rate has changed. He takes a random sample of $30$ of the $100$ total parts painted one day to test $H_0:p=0.2$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.2$, where $p$ is the proportion of all parts that the factory painted with the error that day.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did Abdullah's sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "He takes a random sample of $30$ of the $100$ total parts painted one day," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=30$ parts, and we expect $20\%$ of them to have the error we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=30(0.20)\\\\
&=6 \ngeq 10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=30(0.80)\\\\
&=24 \geq 10 
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $6$ errors in the sample, which is fewer than $10$, the sample does **not** satisfy the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, we can still treat each observation in the sample as independent as long as we sample less than $10\%$ of the population. Since our sample of $30$ parts is more than $10\%$ of the $100$ parts painted that day, the sample has **not** met the independence condition.

###Answer

The data is a random sample from the population of interest.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
$np \geq 10$ and $n(1-p) \geq 10$
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Leanne manages an apartment building that houses $100$ residents. She read an article claiming that $15\%$ of people in their state were senior citizens, and she thought that it may be higher for residents in her building. She took a random sample of $20$ residents, and she wants to test $H_0:p=0.15$ versus $H_\text{a}:p > 0.15$, where $p$ is the proportion of residents that are senior citizens.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did Leanne's sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "She took a random sample of $20$ residents," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=20$ residents, and we expect $15\%$ of them to be senior citizens we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=20(0.15)\\\\
&=3 \ngeq 10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=20(0.85)\\\\
&=17 \geq 10 
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $3$ senior citizens in the sample, which is fewer than $10$, the sample does **not** satisfy the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, we can still treat each observation in the sample as independent as long as we sample less than $10\%$ of the population. Since our sample of $20$ residents is more than $10\%$ of the $100$ residents in the building, the sample has **not** met the independence condition.

###Answer

The data is a random sample from the population of interest.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The expected counts of successes and failures are both sufficiently large.
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Brady manages a small airline, and their goal is for $90\%$ of passengers to say they are satisfied after a flight. Suppose he took a random sample of $10$ passengers from a flight of $50$ passengers to test the hypotheses $H_0:p=0.90$ versus $H_\text{a}:p < 0.90$, where $p$ is the proportion of passengers on that flight who were satisfied.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did Brady's sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...he took a random sample of $10$ passengers from a flight of $50$ passengers," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=10$ passengers, and we expect $90\%$ of them to be satisfied we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=10(0.90)\\\\
&=9 \ngeq 10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=10(0.10)\\\\
&=1 \ngeq 10 
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $9$ satisfied customers and $1$ not satisfied customer, both of which are fewer than $10$, the sample does **not** satisfy the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, we can still treat each observation in the sample as independent as long as we sample less than $10\%$ of the population. Since our sample of $10$ passengers is more than $10\%$ of the $50$ passengers on that flight, the sample has **not** met the independence condition.

###Answer

The data is a random sample from the population of interest.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
$np \geq 10$ and $n(1-p) \geq 10$
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Moussa saw a commercial on television that claimed $9$ out of $10$ dentists recommend using a specific brand of chewing gum. He suspected that the true proportion was actually lower, so he took an [[☃ definition 1]] of $50$ dentists and asked whether or not they recommend using that brand of chewing gum. He wants to use the sample data to test $H_0:p=0.9$ versus $H_\text{a}:p<0.9$, where $p$ is the proportion of all dentists that recommend this brand of chewing gum.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did Moussa's sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...he took an [[☃ definition 2]] of $50$ dentists...," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=50$ dentists, and we expect $90\%$ of them to recommend the chewing gum, we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=50(0.90)\\\\
&=45 \geq 10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=50(0.10)\\\\
&=5 \ngeq 10 
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $5$ dentists, which is fewer than $10$, to not recommend the chewing gum, the sample does **not** satisfy the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since our sample of $50$ dentists is less than $10\%$ of the population (there are certainly more than $500$ dentists in the population), the sample satisfies the independence condition.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The expected counts of successes and failures are both sufficiently large.
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
A restaurant chain wants to hire roughly the same proportion of men and women for entry-level positions. Management took an [[☃ definition 1]] of $12$ entry-level hires from over $1000$ entry-level hires in a recent month. They want to use the sample data to test $H_0:p=0.5$ versus $H_\text{a}:p\neq0.5$, where $p$ is the proportion of all entry-level hires that month who were women.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did the sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "Management took an [[☃ definition 1]] of $12$ entry-level hires...," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=12$ hires, and we expect $50\%$ of them to be women, we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=12(0.5)\\\\
&=6 \ngeq 10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=12(0.5)\\\\
&=6 \ngeq 10 
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $6$ successes and $6$ failures, both of which are fewer than $10$, the sample does **not** satisfy the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since our sample of $12$ hires is less than $10\%$ of the population (there were over $1000$ entry-level hires that month), the sample satisfies the independence condition.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
$np \geq 10$ and $n(1-p) \geq 10$
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lian read a report claiming that $9\%$ of people in her country were classified as living in poverty. She wonders if this holds true in her large city, so she obtains an [[☃ definition 1]] of $50$ people from her city. She wants to use the sample data to test $H_0:p=0.09$ versus $H_\text{a}:p\neq0.09$, where $p$ is the proportion of all people in her city that are classified as living in poverty.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did Lian's sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...she obtains an [[☃ definition 2]] of $50$ people from her city," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=50$ people, and we expect $9\%$ of them to be classified as living in poverty, we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=50(0.09)\\\\
&=4.5 \ngeq 10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=50(0.09)\\\\
&=45.5 \geq 10 
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $4.5$ people in the sample to be classified as living in poverty, which is fewer than $10$, the sample does **not** satisfy the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since our sample of $50$ people is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says this is large city, so there are certainly more than $500$ people in the city), the sample satisfies the independence condition.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The expected counts of successes and failures are both sufficiently large.
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nala wrote an app that has both a desktop and a mobile version. She wonders which platform is more common, so she takes an [[☃ definition 1]] of $8$ users from over $1000$ active users. She wants to use the sample data to test $H_0:p=0.5$ versus $H_\text{a}:p\neq0.5$, where $p$ is the proportion of all users who are using the mobile version.

**Which conditions for performing this type of test did Nala's sample meet?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...she takes an [[☃ definition 3]] of $8$ users..." so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=8$ users, and we expect $50\%$ of them to use the mobile version, we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=8(0.5)\\\\
&=4 \ngeq 10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=8(0.5)\\\\
&=4 \ngeq 10 
\end{align}$

Since we only expect $4$ successes and $4$ failures, both of which are fewer than $10$, the sample does **not** satisfy the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since our sample of $8$ users is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says there are over $1000$ active users), the sample satisfies the independence condition.

###Answers

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
$np \geq 10$ and $n(1-p) \geq 10$
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a very large poll in 2015, about $90\%$ of homes in California had access to the internet. Market researchers wonder if that proportion is now higher, so they take a random sample of $200$ homes in California and find that $196$ have access to the internet. They want to use this sample data to test $H_0:p=0.9$ versus $H_\text{a}:p>0.9$, where $p$ is the proportion of California homes with internet access.

**Which conditions for performing this type of significance test have been met?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...they take a random sample of $200$ homes in California...," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=200$ homes, and we expect $90\%$ of them to have internet access, we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=200(0.90)\\\\
&=180 \geq10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=200(0.10)\\\\
&=20 \geq 10 
\end{align}$

Even though the sample showed $196$ homes with internet access and $4$ without, we are only concerned with the expected counts. Since $180$ and $20$ are both greater than or equal to $10$, the sample satisfies the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since our sample of $200$ homes is less than $10\%$ of the population (there are certainly more than $2{,}000$ homes in the population), the sample satisfies the independence condition.

###Answer

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* $np \geq 10$ and $n(1-p) \geq 10$
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
$np \geq 10$ and $n(1-p) \geq 10$
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alden claims he is psychic. His friend designs a game to test his psychic abilities. The friend will present Alden with four different cards and ask him to identify which card has a certain picture on it. Only one card has the correct picture, and the friend will randomly assign the position of the correct picture on each trial. So they're willing to assume that Alden has a $25\%$ chance of guessing correctly on any given trial.

They plan to carry out $60$ trials of this experiment to test $H_0:p=0.25$ versus $H_\text{a}:p>0.25$, where $p$ is the proportion of trials that Alden would identify the correct card.

**Which conditions for performing this type of significance test have been met?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "...the friend will randomly assign the position of the correct picture on each trial...," so it seems that they properly randomized the experiment.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=60$ trials, and we expect Alden to be correct on $25\%$ of the trials, we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=60(0.25)\\\\
&=15 \geq10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=60(0.75)\\\\
&=45 \geq 10 
\end{align}$

Since $15$ and $45$ are both greater than or equal to $10$, the sample satisfies the normal condition.

###The independence condition

It seems reasonable to assume independence between trials since the position of the correct card is randomly assigned on each trial (the problem also says they're willing to assume that Alden has a $25\%$ chance of guessing correctly on any given trial).

We don't need to check the $10\%$ condition since we're not sampling from a fixed population without replacement in this context.

###Answer

* They properly randomized the experiment.
* $np \geq 10$ and $n(1-p) \geq 10$
* Each trial can be considered independent.

choices.content: They properly randomized the experiment.
$np \geq 10$ and $n(1-p) \geq 10$
Each trial can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
A large poll showed that $24\%$ of US voters identified as Republicans. Mabel is a political analyst who suspects that this figure is lower in her state. She plans on taking a random sample of $250$ voters in her state to test $H_0:p=0.24$ versus $H_\text{a}:p<0.24$, where $p$ is the proportion of voters in Mabel's state who identify as Republican.

**Which conditions for performing this type of significance test have been met?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "She plans on taking a random sample of $250$ voters in her state...," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=250$ voters, and we expect $24\%$ of them to identify as Republican, we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=250(0.24)\\\\
&=60 \geq10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=250(0.76)\\\\
&=190 \geq 10 
\end{align}$

Since $60$ and $190$ are both greater than or equal to $10$, the sample satisfies the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since our sample of $250$ voters is less than $10\%$ of the population (there are certainly more than $2{,}500$ voters in the state), the sample satisfies the independence condition.

###Answer

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* The expected counts of successes and failures are both sufficiently large.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The expected counts of successes and failures are both sufficiently large.
Individual observations can be considered independent.

assessment_items.question_content:
Quintin read a report claiming that $4\%$ of police officers in the US have had a formal complaint filed against them. Quintin suspects that this figure is higher in his state. He obtains a random sample of records for $500$ officers from the approximately $20{,}000$ officers in his state. He wants to use these data to test $H_0:p=0.04$ versus $H_\text{a}:p>0.04$, where $p$ is the proportion of officers in Quintin's state who have had a formal complaint filed against them.

**Which conditions for performing this type of significance test have been met?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The random condition

The problem reads, "He obtains a random sample of records for $500$ officers...in his state," so we can assume that the sampling method satisfies the random condition.

###The normal condition

The normal condition for this test says that we need at least $10$ expected successes and $10$ expected failures in our sample data when the null hypothesis is true. Since the sample size is $n=500$ officers, and we expect $4\%$ of them to have a formal complaint, we have:

$\begin{align}
\text{expected successes} &= np\\\\
&=500(0.04)\\\\
&=20 \geq10 \\\\\\
\text{expected failures} &= n(1-p)\\\\
&=500(0.96)\\\\
&=480 \geq 10 
\end{align}$

Since $20$ and $480$ are both greater than or equal to $10$, the sample satisfies the normal condition.

###The independence condition

The independence condition says that when sampling without replacement, our sample size shouldn't exceed $10\%$ of the population size. Since our sample of $500$ officers is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says there are approximately $20{,}000$ officers in his state), the sample satisfies the independence condition.

###Answer

* The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
* The expected counts of successes and failures are both sufficiently large.
* Individual observations can be considered independent.

choices.content: The data is a random sample from the population of interest.
The expected counts of successes and failures are both sufficiently large.
Individual observations can be considered independent.
